findings.title: Cascade: Single room failure closes SSE stream for entire job session
evidence: In `ProcessHdrGroupUseCase`, an exception during HDR fusion triggers `await self.event_publisher.publish_progress(session_id, room, "FAILED")`.
In `main.py`, `stream_hdr_progress` listens to the session channel and executes `if message.get("status") in ("COMPLETED", "FAILED", "CANCELLED"): break`, which terminates the SSE generator.

If a session contains 10 rooms and room 1 fails, the SSE connection is permanently closed. The frontend `ProcessingConsole` receives `FAILED` but the server drops the connection, triggering an `onerror` event in the browser.
The frontend `onerror` handler calls `onComplete()` without arguments, instantly jumping to the REVIEW state with fake placeholder data, abandoning the 9 rooms still processing in the background.

findings.title: Composition Failure: Missing terminal signal causes infinite hang and mock data fallback
evidence: In `ProcessHdrGroupUseCase`, a successful room processing ends with publishing `PENDING_CORRECTION` and enqueueing perspective correction.
`CloudTasksAdapter.enqueue_perspective_correction` is a `pass` stub.
No component in the backend ever publishes `COMPLETED` or `FINISHED` to the Redis Pub/Sub channel.
The frontend `ProcessingConsole` waits indefinitely for `COMPLETED`. Eventually, the reverse proxy or browser times out the idle SSE connection, triggering `onerror`.
This forces the frontend to call `onComplete()` with no arguments, meaning the user will NEVER see real processed photos even when the backend perfectly executes the job.

findings.title: Contract Mismatch: Hardcoded JPEG Content-Type silently rejects valid image uploads
evidence: Frontend `handleDrop` allows any file where `file.type.startsWith('image/')` (e.g., `image/png`, `image/webp`).
Backend `GCSBlobStorageAdapter.generate_upload_urls` hardcodes `content_type="image/jpeg"` when generating the V4 signed URL.
Frontend blindly executes `fetch(url, { method: "PUT", headers: { "Content-Type": file.type }, body: file })`.
GCS rejects non-JPEG uploads with `403 SignatureDoesNotMatch`. The frontend `Promise.all` does not check `res.ok`, silently swallowing the error.
Frontend signals `/api/jobs/{sessionId}/finalize`, instructing the backend to process blobs that were never successfully uploaded, causing downstream pipeline crashes.

findings.title: Resource Exhaustion Cascade: Synchronous malloc_trim freezes concurrent event loop
evidence: FastAPI operates on an asynchronous event loop handling multiple concurrent requests (SSE heartbeats, Cloud Tasks webhooks).
`ProcessHdrGroupUseCase` executes `ctypes.CDLL('libc.so.6').malloc_trim(0)` synchronously in its `finally` block for every processed room.
`malloc_trim(0)` pauses all threads in the Python process to return freed memory to the OS.
Under load, this repeatedly blocks the event loop, causing SSE connections to drop and Cloud Tasks requests to timeout.
Timeouts cause Cloud Tasks to retry the HTTP requests, creating a retry storm that permanently exhausts the server's processing capacity.

findings.title: State Corruption: Unsafe filename sanitization overwrites exposure brackets
evidence: User uploads files with special characters (e.g., `IMG@001.JPG` and `IMG#001.JPG`) or identical names from different folders.
Frontend only sends `file.name` to `/api/upload-urls`.
Backend `GCSBlobStorageAdapter` strips non-alphanumeric characters (`re.sub(r'[^a-zA-Z0-9_.-]', '', file)`), causing both files to resolve to `session_id/IMG001.JPG`.
Backend returns two signed URLs pointing to the exact same GCS destination.
Frontend concurrently PUTs two different brackets to the same path. One overwrites the other, silently corrupting the HDR fusion pipeline by omitting necessary exposure data.

findings.title: Abuse Case: Unbounded concurrent uploads exhaust client connections
evidence: Frontend `handleDrop` iterates over all dropped files and executes `Promise.all(files.map(file => fetch(...)))`.
If a user drops 500 exposures, the browser attempts to open 500 concurrent HTTP PUT requests.
This exhausts the browser's connection pool (typically 6 per domain), spikes local memory as files are read, stalls network queues, and risks GCS `429 Too Many Requests`.
Because the `fetch` calls lack error boundary checks for `res.ok`, dropped requests silently fail.
